title: Scientists Solve Dinosaur Egg Mystery with a Model Nest
summary: Scientists in Taiwan built a life-size model of an oviraptor dinosaur and its nest to learn how these creatures hatched their eggs 70 million years ago. Oviraptors were bird-like but flightless dinosaurs that lived in China. They laid eggs in rings in open nests. The researchers used materials like foam and resin to make the model and tested how heat moved through the eggs. They found that when an adult dinosaur was present in cold weather, eggs in the outer ring got much cooler, which could cause them to hatch at different times. In warm weather, sunlight helped keep temperatures more even. This study shows that oviraptors probably used sunlight to warm their eggs, unlike modern birds that sit on them. It helps solve a long mystery about dinosaur parenting.

Where did oviraptors live millions of years ago?
How were oviraptor eggs arranged in the nest?
What helped keep egg temperatures even in warm weather?
What could happen to eggs in cold weather with an adult present?
How do modern birds warm their eggs compared to oviraptors?

background_read: Oviraptors were dinosaurs that lived about 70 million years ago. They were similar to birds but could not fly. Scientists have been curious about how they cared for their eggs, since fossils show they laid them in open nests. By building a model, researchers can test ideas without needing real dinosaurs, helping us understand ancient life better.
Article_Structure: WHO: Scientists in Taiwan led by Dr. Tzu-Ruei Yang and Chun-Yu Su
WHAT: Built a model nest to study how oviraptor dinosaurs hatched their eggs
WHERE: In Taiwan, based on fossils from China
WHY: To solve the mystery of whether oviraptors used heat from their bodies or the sun to incubate eggs

perspectives.description: Using models to learn about extinct animals helps us understand dinosaur behavior and evolution.
